channel.title: KillBait - News highlights delivered clearly and responsibly—no clickbait, no sensationalism.
channel.description: KillBait is a news aggregator developed by students and professionals in journalism and web development from Argentina, Spain, and the United Kingdom. Our focus is on providing readers with clear, concise, and relevant summaries of each news story.

item.title: What is KillBait?
item.description: Surely you've clicked on a news article with an eye-catching or sensational headline...

item.title: How KillBait works
item.description: Our editors, along with any registered user, can submit news they find interesting simply by providing the URL to the system.

item.title: Frecuently Asked Questions
item.description: Common Questions about KillBait

item.description: Best media: This classification is based on the votes cast by the users of this website on each news item from different media, and it does not necessarily reflect reality.

item.description: Worst media: This classification is based on the votes cast by the users of this website on each news item from different media, and it does not necessarily reflect reality.

item.title: Upcoming news
European Nations Debate Responses to Surging Energy Costs Amid Global Tensions
item.description: European countries are confronting sharp disagreements over how to address skyrocketing energy prices triggered by ongoing conflicts in the Middle East and the lingering effects of the Ukraine war. Gas prices in Europe have surged to levels not seen since 2023, sparking heated debates over energy regulation. Central to the discussion is the EU's 'merit-order' electricity pricing system, which often makes renewable energy appear more expensive than it should be. Heavy industries are pushing for reforms, claiming that the current system no longer fits a crisis heavily influenced by fossil fuels, while power producers insist that the existing framework guarantees fair pricing and investment incentives. Some governments, particularly Germany and Italy, are exploring temporary relaxations of carbon regulations to reduce energy costs, although such measures face resistance from environmental advocates and EU green policy proponents. Efforts to increase renewable energy usage face long timeli

item.title: Former Israeli Prime Minister Warns of Strategic Threat from Turkey
item.description: Former Israeli Prime Minister Naftali Bennett recently issued strong statements regarding Turkey and its president, Recep Tayyip Erdogan, labeling Ankara as a new strategic threat to Israel's security. Bennett accused Turkey of supporting not only Iran but also various groups in the Middle East that Israel considers terrorist organizations. In an interview, he described Erdogan as a sophisticated and dangerous adversary aiming to encircle Israel. Bennett urged Israel and its allies to take Ankara’s actions seriously and to implement a comprehensive containment strategy, emphasizing that measures should not be limited to Iran alone but should include systemic actions against Turkey. He also highlighted the influence of certain political and financial networks, pointing to Qatar and Turkey’s activities in Syria and Gaza, which he claims strengthen connections tied to the Muslim Brotherhood. Bennett suggested that Israel might consider formally recognizing Turkey as a hostile state. His c

item.title: Dubai Faces Shortages in Fresh Food Supplies Amid Regional Tensions
item.description: Dubai, a major hub in the Gulf region, is facing a temporary shortage of fresh food supplies due to disruptions in global logistics and heightened geopolitical tensions. Stefan Paul, director of Kuehne & Nagel, one of the world's largest logistics companies, reported that the city has fresh food reserves for only about ten days. While this does not indicate an immediate risk of famine, it highlights the vulnerability of cities that rely heavily on imported fresh produce. Approximately 70% of food shipments to Gulf countries pass through the Strait of Hormuz, and recent restrictions have reduced air cargo capacity to the Middle East by 22% between February 28 and March 3. The Jebel Ali port, responsible for serving 50 million people and most of Dubai's perishable imports, briefly suspended operations, affecting the supply of fruits, vegetables, dairy, and herbs. Although Dubai has substantial reserves of dry goods, frozen products, and strategic grains, the city is currently facing the

item.title: The Growing Vulnerability of Data Centers in Military Conflicts
item.description: Recent attacks in Iran have highlighted the strategic vulnerability of data centers in modern warfare. As critical infrastructure for both economic and governmental operations, these facilities are increasingly targeted during conflicts. In the Gulf region, U.S. and Israeli attacks prompted retaliatory strikes by Iran, leading to the destruction of three Amazon Web Services (AWS) data centers—two in the United Arab Emirates and one in Bahrain—causing temporary disruptions in digital services. Although these attacks did not immediately halt operations, AWS advised clients to relocate workloads to other countries to maintain service continuity. Iran has long anticipated such risks, relocating portions of its digital infrastructure underground to create fortified data centers, including the National Iranian Network (NIN), which allows the government to maintain essential services during external attacks. Globally, other countries have adopted similar measures. In the United States, a data

item.title: Machine Learning Identifies Key Indicator of Fast Ion Movement in Solid-State Batteries
item.description: Researchers have developed a machine learning approach that could significantly advance the development of all-solid-state batteries (ASSBs), which are safer and potentially more energy-dense than conventional lithium-ion batteries. A major challenge in these batteries is identifying materials that allow ions to move rapidly through solid electrolytes. Traditional experimental and computational methods are often slow or computationally expensive, particularly for systems where ions move in a liquid-like manner. The new workflow combines machine learning force fields with tensorial models to simulate Raman spectra, revealing a distinctive low-frequency signal that corresponds to rapid ion motion disrupting crystal symmetry. This signal provides a clear indicator of high ionic mobility. The approach was validated using sodium-ion conducting materials like Na3SbS4, confirming that strong low-frequency Raman features correlate with fast ionic conduction, while materials with hopping-based

item.title: Two Arrested After Suspicious Devices Thrown During Protests Outside NYC Mayor’s Residence
item.description: During protests outside Gracie Mansion, the official residence of New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, tensions between opposing groups escalated, resulting in the deployment of ignited suspicious devices. The incident occurred amid an anti-Islam protest led by right-wing influencer and pardoned January 6 rioter Jake Lang, attended by roughly 20 people, and a counter-protest titled 'Drive the Nazis Out of New York,' which drew approximately 125 participants. According to NYPD Commissioner Jessica S. Tisch, one counter-protester, 18, lit and threw a device toward the protest area, and later retrieved and ignited a second device with the assistance of a 19-year-old. Both men were arrested along with four others for related offenses, including the use of pepper spray and disorderly conduct. The devices, described as jars wrapped in black tape containing bolts, screws, and a hobby fuse, were analyzed by the bomb squad and sent for further testing. No injuries were reported, and the mayor an

item.title: Addressing the Needs of Single Employees in Family-Focused Workplaces
item.description: Modern workplaces have increasingly adapted to support dual-career couples and working parents by providing flexible hours, paid parental leave, and childcare subsidies. While these measures are beneficial, they often overlook a significant portion of the workforce: single employees without dependents. Currently, nearly half of U.S. adults are unmarried, and single-person households are the most common living arrangement. Yet, many HR policies still assume a traditional family structure, favoring married employees or those raising children. This can manifest in scheduling expectations, benefits allocation, and leave policies that disadvantage singles, who may be expected to work holidays, travel more, or take less desirable vacation times. Moreover, benefits like health coverage, retirement contributions, and bereavement leave often provide more value to married employees, while singles’ broader support networks, including chosen family, are rarely recognized. Experts suggest that comp

item.title: Alphabet Approves Performance-Based Compensation Package for CEO Sundar Pichai Worth Up to $692 Million Over Three Years
item.description: Alphabet, Google's parent company, has approved a new three-year compensation package for CEO Sundar Pichai that could total up to $692 million, according to an SEC filing reported on March 7, 2026. The package keeps his base salary unchanged at $2 million per year (totaling $6 million over three years) and eliminates any annual bonus. The vast majority of the potential payout comes from equity awards, including restricted stock units valued at $84 million that vest gradually over the period as long as he remains with the company. Additional performance stock units are tied to Alphabet's total shareholder return compared to S&P 100 peers. Notably, this deal introduces new incentives linked to the performance and valuation growth of Alphabet's 'Other Bets' subsidiaries, specifically Waymo (autonomous vehicles) and Wing (drone delivery), marking a first for tying CEO pay directly to these ventures' success. This structure could position Pichai as one of the highest-paid executives global

item.title: Obama Delivers Eulogy at Rev. Jesse Jackson's Final Chicago Memorial
item.description: Former President Barack Obama delivered a moving eulogy at the final public tribute to civil rights icon Rev. Jesse Jackson held at a Chicago church on March 6, 2026. Jackson, who passed away at age 84 last month after battling a rare neurological disorder, was honored by Obama, former Presidents Joe Biden and Bill Clinton, and former Vice President Kamala Harris. Obama credited Jackson's groundbreaking 1980s presidential campaigns with paving the way for Black leaders, including his own path to the presidency, stating, 'He paved the road for so many others to follow' and emphasizing Jackson's courage and audacity. He reflected on Jackson's message of empowerment to the overlooked and dispossessed, saying Jackson stepped forward repeatedly when the poor needed a champion. Obama noted the challenging times, remarking, 'We are living in a time when it can be hard to hope,' amid assaults on democratic institutions, yet urged carrying forward Jackson's legacy of hope and service. Attendees

item.title: Economist Proposes Measuring Poverty as a Continuous Spectrum to Better Capture Inequality's Impact
item.description: An economist critiques the traditional use of fixed poverty lines in the United States, which have remained largely unchanged since the 1960s. The piece references Wall Street investor Michael W. Green's late-2025 argument to raise the poverty threshold for a family of four from about $33,000 to $140,000 annually, which generated widespread media discussion. However, the author argues that debates over specific thresholds—whether $33,000, $100,000, or $140,000—miss the point, as no single arbitrary line truly separates poverty from adequacy. Instead, poverty should be viewed as a spectrum, where lower income always means greater hardship without a magical cutoff. The author introduces 'average poverty,' a measure defined as the inverse of average income (expressed in days, hours, or minutes per dollar earned, adjusted for purchasing power). This approach reveals that the U.S. requires an average of 63 minutes to earn $1, far higher than in countries like the UK (34 minutes), France (un

item.title: Britney Spears arrested in California on suspicion of driving under the influence
item.description: Britney Spears, the 44-year-old pop singer, was arrested in California on Wednesday evening for allegedly driving under the influence of a combination of drugs and alcohol. Police reported that Spears was driving a black BMW 430i erratically at high speed on southbound US-101 near Newbury Park. Officers observed signs of impairment and conducted a series of field sobriety tests before taking her into custody. Spears spent the night in Ventura County Main Jail and was released around 6 a.m. local time. She is scheduled to appear in court on May 4. Following the incident, Spears’ Instagram account, which had more than 42 million followers, was deactivated. Her representative described the incident as 'completely inexcusable' and emphasized that Spears plans to comply with the law and take steps toward positive change, including receiving support from loved ones. This is not Spears’ first legal issue related to driving; in 2007, she avoided a criminal hit-and-run trial by paying for damag

item.title: Kristi Noem Removed from Position, Markwayne Mullin Appointed
item.description: In a recent development in U.S. political circles, Kristi Noem, previously nicknamed 'ICE Queen' and 'ICE Barbie,' has been removed from her position, reportedly by order of former President Donald Trump. Noem's departure paves the way for Markwayne Mullin to assume the role she vacated. This change is part of a broader effort by Trump to reorganize and consolidate influence within his circle. Meanwhile, Trump has expressed his desire to play a role in selecting the next Iranian Supreme Leader, highlighting ongoing international interests and tensions. Mark Stone, reporting from Corpus Christi, Texas, is gathering opinions from Americans and Trump supporters regarding the war in Iran. Additionally, Argentine soccer star Lionel Messi visited the White House, meeting with the president in a separate event that drew public attention. The news has been covered as part of the Trump100 podcast series, which provides updates on political maneuvers, international matters, and high-profile pers

item.title: OpenAI's Head of Robotics Resigns Over Concerns Regarding Pentagon AI Agreement
item.description:
Caitlin Kalinowski, who led OpenAI's robotics team after joining from Meta in November 2024, announced her resignation on March 7, 2026, citing ethical concerns related to the company's recent agreement with the U.S. Department of Defense. In social media posts, she expressed that while AI plays a vital role in national security, issues such as warrantless surveillance of Americans and fully autonomous lethal weapons required more thorough discussion and safeguards than were provided. She described her departure as a matter of principle rather than personal conflict, maintaining respect for CEO Sam Altman and the team, and later clarified her primary issue was the rushed nature of the announcement without properly defined guardrails, framing it as a governance problem.
The Pentagon deal, announced shortly after failed negotiations with rival Anthropic (which sought strict limits on domestic surveillance and autonomous weapons, leading to its designation as a supply-chain risk), allows

item.title: Democrats Assess Opportunity in Texas’ 23rd District After Gonzales Withdraws
item.description: Texas’ 23rd Congressional District may become unexpectedly competitive following Rep. Tony Gonzales’ decision to withdraw from his reelection campaign amid a personal scandal. Gonzales’ exit has left Brandon Herrera, a social media personality and gun rights advocate known as “TheAKGuy,” as the Republican nominee. Herrera’s controversial online presence, which includes past Nazi imagery and membership in the Sons of Confederate Veterans, has drawn criticism and could be a vulnerability in the general election. Democrats see an opening in a district historically held by Republicans but recently showing signs of changing demographics, including strong Latino voter turnout in recent primaries. Katy Padilla Stout, the Democratic nominee, could leverage these factors to compete for a seat that Trump won by 17 points in 2024 but that Clinton narrowly carried in 2016. While national Democratic organizations have not yet committed resources to the district, local party leaders are optimistic t

item.title: Trump Praises and Imitates Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum's Voice During Summit Remarks on Cartels
item.description: At the Shield of the Americas summit held on March 7, 2026, at his Trump National Doral golf club in Florida, President Donald Trump addressed a group of Latin American leaders on issues including drug cartel violence originating from Mexico. During his remarks, Trump expressed admiration for Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum, describing her as a 'very good person,' a 'beautiful woman,' and someone with a 'beautiful voice.' He then performed a breathy vocal imitation of her, repeating phrases like 'President, president, president' and 'No, no, please, president' to mimic her alleged response to his earlier suggestion of deploying U.S. troops to eradicate Mexican cartels. Sheinbaum, who was not present at the event, has previously rejected such U.S. military intervention proposals. Trump's comments occurred amid broader discussions at the summit about launching a coalition against drug cartels, with Trump emphasizing the need to combat what he called the 'epicenter' of criminal violen

item.title: Spain Partially Evacuates Personnel from Tehran Embassy Amid Iran Conflict
item.description: In response to escalating tensions in the Iran conflict during early March 2026, Spain has taken measures to ensure the safety of its diplomatic personnel in Tehran. The Wall Street Journal live coverage reports that Spain evacuated non-essential staff from its embassy in Tehran, while the ambassador and a minimal team remain to maintain operations. This action aligns with broader international concerns over the ongoing war involving strikes between Israel, the US, and Iran, which has led to attacks on diplomatic missions across the Middle East. Spanish Foreign Minister José Manuel Albares coordinated the evacuation, similar to prior operations in 2025 and early 2026 that successfully relocated dozens of Spanish citizens from Iran via land convoys to neighboring countries like Armenia and Azerbaijan. The embassy continues to function at a reduced capacity, providing assistance to remaining Spaniards in Iran, who number fewer than 200 following multiple evacuation waves. Albares praised

item.title: The Risks of U.S. Support for Iranian Kurdish Opposition Forces
item.description: As the Iranian regime faces significant pressure from U.S. and Israeli military action, Washington has reportedly explored the idea of supporting Kurdish opposition groups in Iran to destabilize the clerical leadership. Iran's Kurds, comprising 8–17% of the population, have a long history of repression and political marginalization. While some Kurdish factions may welcome the fall of the Islamic Republic, U.S. support carries major ethical, strategic, and humanitarian risks. Past U.S. interventions with Kurdish groups, including the abandonment of Iraqi Kurds in 1975, demonstrate the dangers of unreliable alliances. Furthermore, arming Kurdish groups like PJAK could escalate tensions with Turkey, a NATO ally that views Kurdish militias as terrorist threats, and could complicate relations in Syria and Iraq. Kurdish forces are fragmented and may lack the capacity to sustain an effective uprising, while Iran’s military has already targeted Kurdish positions. Any insurgency would likely en

item.title: U.S. Customs and Border Protection Purchased Phone Location Data from Online Ads
item.description: The United States Customs and Border Protection (CBP) has admitted to purchasing phone location data from the online advertising industry, marking a significant expansion of government surveillance capabilities. This data, obtained through real-time ad auctions, allows tracking of individuals' daily movements. The purchase occurred during a trial between 2019 and 2021. Meanwhile, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) reportedly plans to access a system called Webloc to monitor mobile phone movements across entire neighborhoods. Other security-related news includes the FBI obtaining information from Proton Mail via Swiss authorities to identify an Atlanta protester, highlighting the distinction between privacy and anonymity. The FBI is also investigating suspicious activity on part of its network handling wiretaps. International law enforcement agencies recently dismantled the Leakbase cybercrime forum, which had over 140,000 paying members. Meta smart glasses have raised priva

item.title: 5 Coffee Shop Etiquette Tips for a Polite and Smooth Experience
item.description: Although coffee shops are often seen as cozy and casual spaces, etiquette expert Lisa Mirza Grotts emphasizes that they are shared public spaces requiring courtesy and awareness. Grotts points out five common habits that customers should avoid to maintain a respectful and efficient environment. First, she advises patrons to approach the counter fully prepared with their drink choices, including size and any customizations, while practicing politeness and eye contact. Second, customers should avoid using their phones while ordering, as this can slow the line and disrupt the flow of service. Third, coffee shops are not personal offices; customers should clean up after themselves, maintain a reasonable conversation volume, and treat staff and other patrons with respect. Fourth, overly complicated drink orders should be moderated during busy hours to ensure smooth service for everyone. Finally, lingering in cafés without making additional purchases is discouraged; a good practice is to buy

item.title: Trump Administration's Legal Opinion Claims Authority to Override California Regulations on Offshore Oil Restart Using Defense Production Act Amid Rising Gas Prices
item.description: Amid surging global fuel prices triggered by the ongoing war in Iran, the U.S. Department of Justice issued a 22-page legal opinion asserting that President Trump can invoke the Defense Production Act of 1950 to preempt California state laws and regulations blocking the restart of offshore oil production by Sable Offshore Corp. The Houston-based company aims to revive dormant infrastructure off the Santa Barbara County coast, including offshore rigs like Platform Hondo and a pipeline network previously shut down after a major 2015 oil spill caused by severe corrosion. The opinion describes the infrastructure as a 'critical energy resource on the West Coast' and argues that a presidential order could override state approvals, local concerns, and even aspects of a prior federal consent decree related to noncompliance issues. Sable has faced repeated setbacks, including lawsuits from California's attorney general and environmental regulators over safety, environmental protections, and all

item.title: BBC Persian Issues Correction After Mistranslating 'Regime' as 'People' in Pete Hegseth's Iran Speech
item.description: The British Broadcasting Corporation's Persian service made a significant translation error during a live broadcast of U.S. Secretary of War Pete Hegseth's recent speech on Iran. Hegseth referred to the Iranian regime being effectively ended through U.S. and Israeli military actions, stating that the regime which once chanted 'death to America and death to Israel' had received 'death from America and death from Israel,' clarifying it was not a traditional regime-change war but the outcome improved global security. However, BBC Persian incorrectly translated 'regime' as 'mardom' (meaning 'people' in Persian), implying the U.S. targeted ordinary Iranians rather than the government. This alteration changed the core message and sparked criticism, including from Persian linguist Thamar Eilam-Gindin, who argued it distorted the intent and fueled perceptions of pro-regime bias at BBC Persian among the Iranian diaspora. The BBC quickly corrected the mistake, attributing it to human error in li

item.title: Melbourne Wedding Reception Showcases a Bold Black and Color Theme
item.description: In Melbourne, Von and Ryan Bishop hosted a wedding reception that uniquely showcased their contrasting personalities through a striking visual design. The event space was divided into two distinct halves: one entirely black, representing Von, and the other bursting with vibrant colors, representing Ryan. This dichotomy extended from the walls to the table settings and place arrangements, creating a cohesive and visually captivating experience. Guests were asked to adhere to either black or colorful attire, which amplified the immersive effect of the design. The bride's dramatic entrance in all black added a theatrical touch to the celebration. The reception stands out not only for its innovative aesthetic but also for its thoughtful representation of individuality and partnership, making it a memorable event for both the couple and their guests. The event has been highlighted by design enthusiasts and lifestyle publications for pushing creative boundaries in wedding planning and décor.

item.title: Landmark Lawsuit Challenges Social Media Platforms' Addictive Design Features Under Product Liability
item.description: A significant trial in a Los Angeles courtroom is examining whether social media platforms like Instagram and YouTube can be held liable for harms caused by their design, rather than user-generated content. The plaintiff, a 20-year-old woman known as K.G.M., alleges she became addicted starting from age 6 on YouTube and age 9 on Instagram due to features such as infinite scroll, autoplay, likes, algorithmic recommendations, and unpredictable rewards—elements compared to slot machines. These allegedly led to her depression, anxiety, body dysmorphia, and suicidal thoughts. TikTok and Snapchat settled out of court, leaving Meta and Google as defendants. This bellwether case, selected as a representative trial, could influence outcomes for about 1,600 related plaintiffs, including over 350 families and 250 school districts, consolidated in California proceedings with linked federal cases. The legal strategy relies on product liability and negligence claims, arguing that design choices are

item.title: Iranian Americans in Los Angeles Shift from Celebration to Concern Over Potential Chaos in Iran After Khamenei's Death
item.description: Following the killing of Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in U.S. and Israeli airstrikes, Iranian American communities in Los Angeles, especially in Westwood's 'Tehrangeles' area, initially erupted in jubilation with thousands taking to the streets to celebrate the fall of the long-standing regime they fled after the 1979 Islamic Revolution. Many exiles, including those who escaped persecution or war, viewed his death as a positive step toward a freer Iran. However, within days, the mood among some shifted to apprehension as the conflict escalated rapidly. Interviewees like software engineer Arin Saghatelian, who fled Iran as a child, expressed relief at the regime's weakening but worried that U.S. involvement could lead to a prolonged, costly quagmire reminiscent of the Iraq War after Saddam Hussein's 2003 ouster, with risks of sectarian violence, ethnic fragmentation, and chaos far worse due to Iran's complex demographics. Former dissident Roozbeh Farahanipour echoed thes

item.title: Explosion at Dali Nightclub in Trujillo, Peru, Injures 33 People Including Three Minors Amid Rising Extortion Violence
item.description: A powerful explosion struck the Dali nightclub in Trujillo, a coastal city in Peru's La Libertad region, in the pre-dawn hours of March 7, 2026, injuring 33 people, authorities reported. The blast occurred while patrons were inside, leading to panic, shrapnel wounds, amputations in some cases, and glass injuries. Among the victims were three minors—one 16-year-old and two 17-year-olds—who were treated at local hospitals. At least five individuals were in serious condition and required surgery, according to Gerardo Florián Gómez, executive director of the Trujillo Health Network. Eyewitness Fiorella Mantilla described hearing a sudden silence after the blast, as if the sound system had cut off, followed by chaos with glass embedded in her legs. The incident took place in an area increasingly affected by organized crime, extortion, and illegal mining activities. Official figures indicate that La Libertad saw 286 explosions in 2025, with 136 in Trujillo alone. This event followed a simila

item.title: Rural Democrats Question Party’s ‘Affordability’ Message Ahead of the Midterms
item.description:
The article examines growing frustration among rural Democratic candidates and strategists who believe their party’s heavy focus on an abstract “affordability” message is missing the mark with voters in sparsely populated communities. While national Democrats argue that an affordability agenda—framing the midterms around high prices, wages, and President Donald Trump’s handling of the economy—will be key to winning back Congress, rural candidates say that slogan has become vague, overused, and disconnected from the daily realities they see on the campaign trail. They argue that simply repeating the word does little to convince skeptical voters that Democrats understand their challenges or are willing to invest in long-shot rural districts.
Several candidates running in deeply Republican areas describe how conversations with voters often begin with basic questions about what party they belong to, forcing them to frame themselves first as Americans before Democrats in order to be heard.

item.title: Life EV Acquires Rad Power Bikes Following Bankruptcy Filing
item.description: Life Electric Vehicles Holdings (Life EV) has officially acquired Rad Power Bikes, including its intellectual property, inventory, and certain operating assets, for $13.2 million. Rad Power Bikes, a prominent electric bicycle company that raised nearly $330 million in venture capital, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in December after struggling financially for several months. Life EV plans to maintain Rad Power Bikes' operations under the existing brand in the United States and expand retail presence in select key markets. The company has assured existing customers that warranties and gift cards will be honored. Based in Florida, Life EV specializes in acquiring, developing, and scaling electric bicycle and micro-mobility brands. The acquisition of Rad Power Bikes aligns with Life EV’s strategy of expanding its footprint across North America. Life EV also holds an equity stake in LEV Manufacturing, Inc., which acquired the Serial 1 premium electric bicycle brand, originally developed b

item.title: British and French Troops Conduct Military Exercises in Ukraine Amid Rising Tensions
item.description: Over 600 British and French paratroopers are currently training together in exercises simulating combat scenarios, ambushes, and defensive operations, preparing for potential involvement in Ukraine. This activity contrasts with the public diplomatic stance of European governments advocating for peace in the region. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has suggested that France and the United Kingdom could each deploy up to 5,000 troops if an agreement with Russia is reached. The UK has already established a 70-person unit with a budget of £200 million aimed at preparing a military contingent for Ukraine, even as its overall army strength remains at a historic low of approximately 70,000 troops. Experts, including former NATO deputy commander General Richard Shirreff, have criticized the U.S. and its allies for engaging in conflicts without clear objectives or strategies, warning that tensions could escalate rapidly. The French government, under President Macron, has committed to mili

item.title: Rare MET Gene Mutation Identified as a Direct Cause of Fatty Liver Disease
item.description: Researchers at Mayo Clinic have discovered a rare mutation in the MET gene that can directly lead to metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease, formerly known as nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. This mutation interferes with the liver's ability to process fat, causing fat accumulation, inflammation, fibrosis, and potentially cirrhosis or liver cancer. The discovery originated from a father-daughter pair who developed severe liver disease despite lacking common risk factors like diabetes or high cholesterol. Extensive genetic analysis revealed a single DNA letter change in the MET gene, disrupting liver fat metabolism. Subsequent examination of the Mayo Clinic Tapestry study, which includes DNA data from over 100,000 participants, found that about 1% of adults with fatty liver disease carried rare MET variants, with nearly 18% located in the same critical region as the original family. The findings highlight how rare genetic variants can underlie common diseases and emph

item.title: Iran Signals Possible De-escalation Amid Continued Strikes and Leadership Uncertainty
item.description: The conflict between Iran and its Gulf neighbors intensified even as Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian delivered what appeared to be the country's first statement hinting at de-escalation. Pezeshkian said Tehran would stop attacks on Gulf states if they refrained from allowing strikes on Iran from their territories. However, mere hours after his announcement, Iran launched a new wave of ballistic missiles and drone strikes, particularly against the United Arab Emirates, marking some of the heaviest bombardments since hostilities began. The attacks coincided with the one-week anniversary of the killing of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei by joint U.S.-Israeli strikes, which triggered widespread chaos and a leadership vacuum in Tehran. Pezeshkian apologized to neighboring states for the destruction caused by Iranian forces and expressed regret over military actions allegedly carried out without full civilian oversight. His comments, though conciliatory in tone, were undermined by simultaneo

item.title: Philosophical Roots of Modern Authenticity: Insights from Rousseau and Heidegger
item.description: The article explores how authenticity has become a central value for today's youth, particularly Gen Z, with a Ernst & Young report showing over 90% prioritizing being true to oneself above other values. This emphasis is heightened in an era dominated by social media and AI, making genuine self-expression challenging. The author questions whether authenticity and sincerity are timeless virtues or modern obsessions tied to technology. Drawing on history of political thought, the piece examines two key philosophers. Jean-Jacques Rousseau critiqued modern commercial society as conformist and hypocritical, where people wear masks of politeness to pursue self-interest, leading to mutual enslavement. He promoted sincerity as a countercultural virtue enabling self-realization and opposition to societal hypocrisy. In contrast, Martin Heidegger, in 'Being and Time,' described inauthentic existence as the 'they-self,' where individuals lose themselves in public conformity and 'uninhibited hustle

item.title: A Nurse’s Journey Through Endometrial Cancer and Life After Hysterectomy
item.description: Casey Halterman, a nurse from South Carolina, shares her personal journey with stage 3A endometrial adenocarcinoma, a type of uterine cancer. In 2020, she felt an unshakable intuition that something was wrong, despite normal bloodwork. Over time, worsening symptoms including fatigue, severe acid reflux, pelvic and abdominal pain, bloating, and irregular periods led her back to a doctor, who quickly referred her to the ER. Imaging revealed a massive eight-pound tumor. Surgical intervention involved a hysterectomy and removal of her ovaries and fallopian tubes, leading to immediate surgical menopause and associated symptoms like hot flashes, insomnia, and depression. Halterman later underwent chemotherapy and pelvic radiation. Her story highlights the rarity of endometrial cancer in younger women, the importance of advocating for one’s health, and the emotional and physical challenges faced after treatment. By sharing her experience online, she has created a supportive community and rais

item.title: Pentagon Appoints Gavin Kliger, Former DOGE Staffer with Controversial Online History, as Chief Data Officer to Oversee AI Initiatives
item.description: On March 7, 2026, the Pentagon appointed Gavin Kliger as its new Chief Data Officer, a position that positions him to lead the department's major artificial intelligence projects. Kliger, a computer scientist, previously worked with Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) initiative in 2025, contributing to government overhaul efforts. The role involves daily coordination of AI projects, collaboration with leading U.S. AI laboratories, and ensuring technology supports military personnel effectively. The announcement highlights the urgency of advancing U.S. military AI amid global competition. However, the appointment has drawn attention due to Kliger's past social media activity. Reports from Reuters indicate that between October 2024 and January 2025, Kliger reposted content from white supremacist Nick Fuentes and self-described misogynist Andrew Tate, and made controversial statements. Kliger denied supporting bigots, extremists, or white supremacists in an email respo

item.title: The Palisadian-Post Newspaper to Relaunch Under New Ownership Following Fire Recovery Efforts
item.description: The nearly century-old Palisadian-Post newspaper in Pacific Palisades, California, is set to relaunch in May under new owners Laura and Tim Schneider after closing due to the devastating Palisades fire in January 2025. The fire destroyed thousands of homes and businesses, leading to a mass loss of advertisers and subscribers, which forced the paper to shut down. The Schneiders, longtime residents and experienced publishers, aim to revitalize the paper as a digital-first platform while preserving its print heritage. The relaunch coincides with the publication’s 98th anniversary and will focus on providing reliable, fact-checked information about rebuilding efforts, local schools, sports, and community events. They also plan to digitize historical archives dating back to 1928, which survived the fire, offering a connection to the community’s past. The couple hopes the revived newspaper will act as a catalyst for community recovery, reconnecting displaced residents and attracting new busi

item.title: Nintendo Files Lawsuit Seeking Refunds With Interest on Tariffs Ruled Unlawful by Supreme Court
item.description: Nintendo of America has filed a lawsuit in the U.S. Court of International Trade against the federal government, demanding refunds plus interest on tariffs it paid under executive orders issued by President Donald Trump. These orders invoked the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose duties on imports from numerous countries starting in early 2025. The Supreme Court later ruled that Trump exceeded his authority in using IEEPA for such broad tariffs, declaring them unlawful. Nintendo's complaint highlights that the company, as an importer of record for its products, suffered direct financial harm from these now-invalid duties. Over a thousand other companies have filed similar suits, with the tariffs reportedly generating more than $200 billion in collections overall. Following the Supreme Court's decision—which Trump criticized as 'extraordinarily anti-American'—he increased certain tariffs from 10% to 15%, leading to additional legal challenges from 24 states al

item.title: California Residents Will Move Clocks Forward as Daylight Saving Time Begins
item.description: On Sunday, Californians will once again adjust their clocks forward by one hour for the start of daylight saving time at 2 a.m. Despite widespread public frustration, legislative efforts to eliminate the twice-yearly time change have repeatedly stalled. Proposition 7, passed in 2018, allows California to adopt permanent daylight saving time if federal law permits, but Congress has yet to act on similar measures. While some lawmakers favor permanent daylight saving time for later sunsets and possible economic benefits, health experts advocate for permanent standard time, citing alignment with circadian rhythms and reduced risk of heart attacks, strokes, and traffic accidents associated with the spring forward. Public opinion polls reveal that a majority of Americans would like to eliminate the clock changes, though there is no consensus on which system should remain year-round. Arizona and Hawaii are the only states to adopt permanent standard time, and federal law currently prevents ot

item.title: Trump Administration Expands ICE Detention with New Warehouse Facilities Amid Reports of Inhumane Conditions
item.description: After being paroled from a California state prison, Gustavo Guevara Alarcon was immediately taken into ICE custody, exposing him to conditions he describes as worse than his 20 years in state prison. Immigration detention, intended to ensure court appearances or deportation compliance, has become punitive under the Trump administration, with overcrowded facilities, poor medical care, inadequate food, and harsh treatment. The administration has used these conditions to pressure migrants into self-deportation and has enacted mandatory detention policies for all noncitizens who entered the U.S. without authorization. ICE has expanded its detention capacity using billions of dollars from Congress, including buying warehouses across the country to serve as mega detention centers capable of holding thousands of people. Private contractors operate most facilities, and lawsuits have been filed over inhumane conditions and medical neglect. Deaths in ICE custody have risen, with reports of impro

item.title: Recommended Lightweight Concealers for a Natural, Minimal Makeup Look
item.description: This HuffPost article highlights the best lightweight concealers ideal for achieving a 'no makeup' makeup appearance that looks fresh and natural without feeling heavy or cakey. Written by Griffin Wynne and published on March 7, 2026, it features recommendations from beauty content creators, influencers, and makeup artists like Layal Assi, Isdianys Araujo, Lauren Peters, and celebrity makeup artist Brett Freedman. The focus is on versatile, easy-to-wear formulas that cover blemishes, dark circles, or uneven tone while maintaining a discreet, skin-like finish suitable for minimal routines. Top picks include Merit Beauty The Minimalist Complexion Stick as an editor favorite for its hybrid foundation-concealer qualities and non-comedogenic properties; Maybelline Instant Age Rewind as a beloved, affordable drugstore option praised for brightening under eyes and lasting wear; Nars Radiant Creamy Concealer as a high-end favorite with wide shade range and no creasing; Urban Decay Quickie for

item.title: US Winter 2025-2026: Record Warmth in the West Overshadows Cold in the East, Ranking Second-Warmest Nationally
item.description: The meteorological winter of December 2025 through February 2026 in the contiguous United States presented a striking regional divide. While much of the eastern US endured prolonged frigid temperatures, heavy snow, and ice—leading many residents to perceive it as an unusually cold season—the western US experienced its warmest winter on record, accompanied by severe snow droughts. Data from multiple sources indicate that the Lower 48 states as a whole recorded its second-warmest winter ever, surpassed only by a few prior seasons. Cities like Salt Lake City, Phoenix, Las Vegas, and Cheyenne set all-time high winter temperature records, and the West faced risks of summer drought and heightened wildfire potential due to low snowpack. In contrast, eastern locations ranked among their colder winters but none with long-term records achieved the absolute coldest. The article highlights that even intense cold outbreaks in populated eastern areas could not offset the widespread warmth elsewhere.

item.title: At Least 8 Killed by Tornadoes from Severe Storms in Central US, Including Michigan and Oklahoma
item.description: Severe thunderstorms spawned multiple destructive tornadoes across parts of the central United States on Thursday and Friday, resulting in at least eight fatalities and numerous injuries. In southwestern Michigan on Friday afternoon, a particularly dangerous tornado, fueled by a lone supercell thunderstorm, struck areas near Three Rivers, Union Lake, and Cass County, killing four people—three in the Union Lake area and one in Cass County—while injuring at least 12 others and causing extensive structural damage, including ripped roofs and flying debris. Local states of emergency were declared, and the Michigan State Emergency Operations Center was activated. Earlier, on Thursday night in western Oklahoma, a tornado killed a mother and her daughter when it struck their vehicle near Fairview, marking the start of the outbreak. On Friday, two more people died in Beggs, Oklahoma, from related storms, though all individuals were later accounted for with no additional missing persons reported

item.title: Escalating US-Israel Conflict with Iran Drives Oil Prices Up and Adds Economic Uncertainty to a Slowing US Economy
item.description: The article analyzes the economic implications of the ongoing war in the Middle East, triggered by joint US and Israeli military strikes on Iran starting February 28, 2026. These actions have caused significant disruptions in global energy supplies, particularly through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for about one-fifth of the world's oil and a third of its natural gas. As a result, crude oil prices have surged by approximately 25%, leading to higher gasoline prices across the United States. Qatar's energy minister issued a stark warning on March 6, 2026, stating that prolonged conflict could 'bring down the economies of the world' due to halted shipping and skyrocketing energy costs. The US economy was already showing weakness, evidenced by an unexpected job loss in February reported on March 6. The author, an economist, highlights the primary risks: inflationary pressures from rising energy costs and slowed growth as uncertainty—described as an 'economic fog of war'—dete

item.title: Investigation Reveals Ukrainian Capital Being Illegally Moved Through Austria and Hungary
item.description: Between 2024 and 2025, authorities in Hungary uncovered a complex network facilitating the illegal transfer of Ukrainian capital through Austria and Hungary. This operation involved fraudulent call centers, fake banking offices, and black-market channels targeting European Union citizens. The network redirected tens of millions of euros through false investments and cryptocurrency schemes. Its operation required a well-established infrastructure, including shell companies, cash channels, and the conversion of some funds into gold. Effective protection appeared to come from political connections, intelligence services, and local police. Hungarian authorities exposed a fully functioning clandestine financial corridor rather than isolated cash smuggling incidents. Key figures, including a general in the Ukrainian security service, were implicated in shielding these fake banking operations. Ukrainian authorities have reportedly attempted to downplay the situation, framing it as routine ban

item.title: Kristi Noem Fired as DHS Secretary Amid Controversy Over Spending and Internal Management
item.description: Kristi Noem, who served as Secretary of Homeland Security, was recently fired by President Trump, though the reasons behind her dismissal were more political than operational. Noem focused heavily on immigration enforcement, reallocating DHS resources away from areas like counterterrorism, disaster relief, and cybersecurity to prioritize operations in sanctuary cities. While this shift did achieve a reduction in illegal border crossings, it sparked widespread protests and incidents, including the controversial deaths of two American citizens during immigration enforcement actions in Minneapolis. In addition to operational criticism, Noem’s $220 million advertising campaign, which featured herself prominently and involved contracts awarded to the husband of a former DHS spokesperson, became a central point of scrutiny by Congress. Despite her abrupt dismissal, Noem maintained composure at the Major Cities Conference in Nashville, addressing law enforcement leaders and reaffirming her co

item.title: ProtonMail Provides User Information to FBI Leading to Arrest of Atlanta Activist
item.description: ProtonMail, a Switzerland-based email provider known for offering secure and private communications, assisted the FBI in identifying and capturing a political activist involved in the Atlanta Forest protests, also called “Stop Cop City.” This activist, linked to alleged acts of arson, vandalism, and doxing, was using the ProtonMail address defendtheatlantaforest@protonmail.com. The FBI requested the information through the Mutual Legal Assistance Treaty (MLAT) with Switzerland. ProtonMail provided details linked to the payment method used for the account, which ultimately led investigators to the individual behind the account. The arrest highlights the limits of privacy even with services marketed as secure, though ProtonMail notes that payments via cryptocurrency or cash can enhance anonymity. The DTAF movement began in 2023 opposing the police plan to clear a forest for new training facilities. This case underscores the intersection of digital privacy, law enforcement, and political

item.title: ALMA Maps Cold Gas Filaments in the Milky Way’s Central Region
item.description: Astronomers using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) have created the most detailed image yet of the Milky Way's central region, known as the Central Molecular Zone (CMZ). Spanning over 650 light-years, the image reveals a dense network of cold gas filaments surrounding the galaxy’s supermassive black hole. This cold gas serves as the raw material for star formation, and the survey, named ALMA CMZ Exploration Survey (ACES), provides unprecedented precision in mapping these structures. The data shows gas formations of varying scales, from massive clouds to smaller clumps near individual stars, as well as a surprisingly complex chemical environment including simple molecules like silicon monoxide and more complex organic compounds such as methanol and acetone. The CMZ hosts some of the Milky Way's most massive stars, many of which are short-lived and end in powerful supernovae or hypernovae. Researchers hope these observations will clarify how stars form in extreme e

item.title: Pakistani Man Convicted for Attempted Assassination Plot Against US Leaders Allegedly Linked to Iran
item.description: Asif Merchant, a 47-year-old Pakistani man, was convicted in a Brooklyn court for plotting to assassinate former U.S. President Donald Trump and other American political figures. Merchant admitted involvement with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps, claiming he participated to protect his family in Tehran. Prosecutors said the plot was connected to revenge for the 2020 killing of Iranian military commander Qassem Soleimani. The plan allegedly targeted Trump, President Joe Biden, and 2024 Republican presidential candidate Nikki Haley. Merchant attempted to recruit individuals within the United States, but the plot was uncovered before any attack could occur. A person he contacted in April 2024 reported him to authorities and became a confidential informant, leading to Merchant's arrest. The U.S. Department of Justice stated Merchant was convicted of 'murder for hire and attempting to commit an act of terrorism transcending national boundaries' at the direction of Iranian official

item.title: X Tests Contextual Ad Format Placing Product Recommendations Under Relevant User Posts
item.description: X, the social media platform formerly known as Twitter, is currently testing a novel advertising approach that places product recommendations directly beneath user posts mentioning a company or its offerings. The test was first noticed in Europe, where a post praising Starlink's satellite internet service in Portugal triggered a 'Get Starlink' prompt below it, linking to the official Starlink website. X's head of product, Nikita Bier, confirmed the experiment and described it as an effort to create 'an ad product that isn’t an ad,' emphasizing a desire for trustworthy, non-deceptive recommendations. He rejected ideas like allowing affiliate links in the slot, noting concerns that it could lead to misinformation. Currently, the ad appears only in select markets, while others see a placeholder box that might display unrelated X content instead. This development comes amid X's broader push to attract more advertisers and creators to the platform, following recent introductions like 'Paid

item.title: Vulnerability of Middle East Desalination Plants to Oil Spills
item.description: Desalination plants in the Middle East are increasingly critical as they provide the majority of drinking water in countries like Kuwait, Oman, and Saudi Arabia, with some plants covering up to 90% of a city's water needs. The proximity of these facilities to the sea and their dependence on electricity make them vulnerable to disruptions from oil spills or other industrial accidents. Recent concerns arose following a potential incident at the Fujairah complex in the UAE, which houses a power plant supporting a major desalination facility, although no fuel leak occurred. The threat of contamination from oil spills has historical precedent, notably during the First Gulf War, prompting improvements in treatment techniques. European companies like Veolia and Suez are heavily involved in the sector, with projects delivering millions of cubic meters of potable water daily and supplying hundreds of thousands of homes. Despite technological advances, the region remains highly dependent on desa

item.title: Reports Suggest Russia Shares Military Intelligence with Iran on U.S. Targets in the Middle East
item.description: According to reports from the Washington Post, Russia is allegedly sharing intelligence with Iran regarding the location of U.S. military assets in the Middle East, including ships and aircraft. U.S. officials describe this as a broad operational effort by Moscow, marking a potential indirect involvement in regional conflicts. Earlier this year, Russia and Iran signed a strategic partnership agreement, strengthening military cooperation between the two nations. Despite these reports, Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth downplayed Russia's role, stating that it is not a significant factor in the ongoing conflict. Iran recently destroyed a U.S. advanced radar system in Jordan, and similar radar facilities in the UAE were reportedly targeted in separate attacks. The loss of these systems has reportedly limited the U.S. military’s early warning capabilities in the region, reducing reaction time against Iranian missile attacks. Videos from Israel suggest that 90% of Iranian missiles are currently h

item.title: Four Dead and Multiple Injuries Reported After Tornadoes Strike Southern Michigan
item.description: Severe storms and at least one confirmed tornado caused widespread devastation in southern Michigan on Friday, March 6, 2026, resulting in four fatalities and numerous injuries. The National Weather Service issued tornado warnings as powerful weather systems moved through the region. In the Union Lake area near Union City in Branch County, an apparent tornado killed three people and injured 12 others, with reports of homes destroyed, roofs torn off buildings, debris scattered, trees uprooted, and vehicles damaged. Videos from residents and social media captured dramatic scenes of whirlwinds ripping through communities, including in nearby Three Rivers. About 50 miles southwest in Cass County, officials confirmed one additional death and several injuries from another tornado. Hundreds of residents were left without power as the storms battered infrastructure. Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer activated the State Emergency Operations Center to monitor the situation and coordinate respon

item.title: Impact of Middle East Conflicts on Global Oil Prices and Economic Stability
item.description: The recent conflict in the Middle East has exposed vulnerabilities in the global economy, particularly in relation to hydrocarbon supply. The International Monetary Fund's director, Kristalina Georgieva, expressed hope for a swift end to the war. Economists warn that rising oil and gas prices passing through the Strait of Hormuz will inevitably trigger inflation. A $30 increase per barrel could raise consumer prices by 2% in the Eurozone and 2.5% in the United States, reducing economic activity by 0.8 and 1.4 points respectively. While historical patterns suggest central banks do not typically react aggressively to oil crises, sustained price increases above $100 per barrel could prompt significant monetary policy adjustments. Additionally, higher long-term interest rates could further strain public finances in Europe and the U.S., where deficits may reach up to 10% of GDP. The ripple effects could devalue the U.S. dollar and impact global financial markets, showing how economic crises

item.title: US Corporate Media's Selective Empathy for Iranians in Coverage of Strikes on Iran
item.description: On February 28, 2026, the United States and Israel launched joint military strikes on Iran, killing Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and targeting nuclear, missile, and security sites, in what was presented as addressing threats but criticized as deliberate provocation amid stalled nuclear negotiations. The article, an opinion piece by Belén Fernández republished on a nuclear issues blog, argues that US corporate media outlets like The New York Times and Washington Post exhibit hypocritical concern for Iranian civilians. They frequently highlight Iranian government oppression—such as crackdowns on protesters, women's rights abuses, and alleged killings—to portray Iranians as victims of their own regime, thereby justifying or rationalizing US/Israeli attacks. However, these same outlets minimize or treat as footnotes the civilian casualties from the strikes themselves, including the deaths of at least 175 people (many children) in a missile hit on a girls' elementary school in Mina

item.title: California Congressman Darrell Issa Announces Retirement, Impacting Republican Hold on House
item.description: California Republican Representative Darrell Issa has announced his retirement, just three months after stating he would not step down, citing the challenges of reelection in a newly redrawn district. Issa, a former chair of the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee and one of Congress's wealthiest members, had been a prominent critic of former President Barack Obama and Secretary of State Hillary Clinton. His decision creates uncertainty in Southern California’s 48th District, now favoring Democrats after recent redistricting. Issa had previously considered running in Texas but chose to remain in California, affirming his commitment to his district. The new congressional map, led by Democratic Governor Gavin Newsom, is designed to flip up to five Republican-held seats to Democrats, countering efforts by former President Donald Trump to gain seats in Texas. Issa endorsed San Diego County Supervisor James Desmond as his successor. The retirement could complicate Republican eff

item.title: US and Israel Conduct Strikes in Iran as Middle East Conflict Escalates
item.description: The conflict between the United States, Israel, and Iran has entered its eighth day, with escalating military actions across the Middle East. Israel launched a broad-scale wave of strikes on Tehran, including areas near Mehrabad Airport, resulting in fires and damage to key infrastructure. The US military has targeted over 3,000 sites in Iran since the conflict began. Iranian state media reports more than 1,230 deaths in Iran, while Israeli strikes in Lebanon have caused over 200 fatalities and displaced nearly half a million people. Gulf states, including Saudi Arabia and the UAE, continue to defend against drone and missile attacks. The war has also impacted civilian life, with disrupted transportation, internet blackouts, and the evacuation of travelers through newly established air corridors. Oil markets are affected, with gas prices spiking amid concerns over supply stability. President Trump has demanded unconditional surrender from Iran, while the UN urges diplomatic solutions t

item.title: Robinhood Ventures Fund Raises $658 Million, Faces Challenges in NYSE Launch
item.description: Robinhood has launched its first startup investment fund, Robinhood Ventures Fund I, aiming to give retail investors access to high-profile private companies. The fund includes stakes in Databricks, Stripe, Mercor, Oura, Ramp, Airwallex, Revolut, and Boom. Despite an ambitious $1 billion target, the fund raised $658.4 million, potentially reaching $705.7 million if underwriters exercise their full allotment. Shares were priced at $25 but closed at $21 on their first day of trading, marking a 16% decline. This contrasts sharply with Destiny Tech100, a publicly traded fund with stakes in companies like SpaceX, OpenAI, and Discord, which saw strong investor demand and premium trading above its net asset value. Analysts suggest Robinhood’s lower demand stems from the fund’s lack of exposure to the most hyped private companies expected to go public at high valuations. Robinhood plans to expand the fund to include 15-20 late-stage growth companies and is exploring opportunities to gain acces

item.title: Milan Cortina Winter Paralympics Open Amid Controversy Over Russian Flag Return and Boycotts
item.description: The Milan Cortina 2026 Winter Paralympics officially opened on March 6 in Verona, Italy, at the historic Arena di Verona, marking the 50th anniversary of the Winter Paralympic Games. The event featured a nations' parade, but was overshadowed by geopolitical tensions, including the return of the Russian flag for the first time since the 2014 Sochi Games. Four Russian athletes participated under their national flag and in bright red uniforms, drawing some boos from the crowd. This signaled a potential full return to Olympic events by 2028. Russia's national anthem could now be played for gold medalists, a first since the 2022 Ukraine invasion. Several countries boycotted the ceremony in protest: Ukraine (whose athletes were absent despite applause during the announcement), Czech Republic, Estonia, Finland, Latvia, Poland, and Lithuania. Belarus, Russia's ally, also returned with two athletes. An Iranian Para cross-country skier withdrew due to travel issues amid escalating Middle East co

item.title: Experts Warn Smart Home Devices Are Vulnerable to Hacking Due to Poor Security Practices
item.description: As homes become smarter with Wi-Fi-connected TVs, speakers, thermostats, lights, and voice assistants controlled via apps, they introduce new cybersecurity risks. While such hacks remain uncommon, experts say they're entirely feasible because many inexpensive smart devices prioritize ease of use over security. Consumers often leave default passwords unchanged or fail to apply firmware updates, making devices easy targets for automated internet scans by attackers. Cybersecurity specialist Dr. Manny Niri from Oxford Brookes University notes that these vulnerabilities allow silent intrusions, like viewing camera feeds unnoticed, as seen in a recent South Korean case where over 120,000 home and business cameras were compromised, leading to arrests. Ethical hacker Glenn Wilkinson compares device security to padlocks, urging better 'locks' through stronger habits. Hackers typically exploit either the devices themselves—small computers with hackable software—or associated cloud services, apps

item.title: 6-Year-Old Deaf Boy and Family Deported to Colombia, Raising Concerns About Medical Care
item.description: A 6-year-old deaf boy from Hayward, California, was deported to Colombia along with his mother and sibling, according to his family attorney, Nikolas De Bremaeker. The attorney claims that U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) intentionally withheld the family's location, preventing legal interventions that might have halted the deportation. The boy, who attends the California School for the Deaf in Fremont, reportedly had no access to his essential hearing devices during detention and after deportation. The mother, Lesly Rodriguez Gutierrez, 28, had a removal order but may have been eligible for other forms of relief. The Department of Homeland Security stated that Rodriguez Gutierrez received due process and chose to be deported with her children. Advocates and state officials, including California Superintendent Tony Thurmond, have criticized ICE’s handling of the case, highlighting the humanitarian and educational impacts on the child. This incident comes amid broader conc

item.title: Iran Faces Widespread Internet Shutdown Amid Conflict and Infrastructure Damage
item.description: Since late February 2026, Iran has experienced a nearly complete internet blackout affecting approximately 90 million people, following U.S. and Israeli airstrikes that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and damaged key telecommunications infrastructure. The Iranian government’s National Information Network (NIN) remains operational, allowing limited domestic connectivity while the general public is mostly cut off from the global internet. Historically, Iranians have circumvented partial shutdowns using VPNs and peer-to-peer platforms like Conduit, but total blackouts prevent most workarounds. Wealthy elites, the government, and some Starlink-enabled gateways retain access, though even these are vulnerable to damage from ongoing airstrikes. The shutdown exacerbates challenges for communication, news dissemination, and evidence collection during the conflict. Meanwhile, regime-linked channels on Telegram remain active, promoting state narratives and selective information. The blackout h

item.title: MPs report wide differences in water companies’ use of bailiffs for debt collection
item.description: New data from a committee of MPs reveals significant variations in how water companies in England and Wales use bailiffs to recover unpaid bills. While some companies, such as Wessex Water, have not employed bailiffs at all in the last decade, others, including Southern Water, have used them thousands of times annually, peaking at over 15,000 instances in 2019. Yorkshire Water, United Utilities, and Severn Trent are also among the companies with frequent bailiff enforcement. The committee’s findings indicate that enforcement peaked in 2023 and 2024, following the COVID-19 pandemic, raising questions about the reasons behind this surge. Water companies claim that bailiffs are a last resort, typically used only when customers are able to pay but choose not to. Vulnerable groups, including the elderly, ill, or those on means-tested benefits, are usually exempt from enforcement, although practices vary between companies. MPs have urged water providers to review their approaches to debt col

item.title: Indonesia to Restrict Social Media Access for Children Under 16
item.description: Indonesia, the world's fourth most populous country, will implement a ban on social media access for children under 16, starting March 28, 2026. The decision targets 'high-risk platforms' including TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, X, YouTube, and Roblox. The government aims to protect young users from online risks such as pornography, cyberbullying, online fraud, and addiction. Indonesia’s Communications and Digital Minister, Meutya Hafid, stated that accounts of under-16s will be gradually deactivated until social media companies meet certain regulatory obligations. The move follows a growing global trend, after countries like Australia, Spain, France, and Italy have introduced similar measures. Social media companies, including Meta and YouTube, expressed support for safer online experiences but emphasized the importance of parental control and avoiding driving teens to less regulated platforms. According to a 2024 survey, nearly 80% of Indonesia’s population uses the internet, including

item.title: BBC apologizes for airing racial slur during BAFTAs ceremony
item.description: The BBC has expressed profound regret over a serious broadcasting error during the BAFTAs on February 22, where a racial slur was aired live as Michael B. Jordan and Delroy Lindo presented an award. The offensive language was uttered involuntarily by John Davidson, a Tourette's campaigner and the inspiration for the film 'I Swear'. Despite being symptomatic of his condition, the slur was broadcast to viewers for over 12 hours before being removed from BBC iPlayer. Outgoing BBC Director General Tim Davie acknowledged the mistake in a letter to Dame Caroline Dinenage, emphasizing that no one in the on-site broadcast truck was aware of the slur at the time, and therefore no editorial decision allowed it to air intentionally. Both Jordan and Lindo continued their presentation after pausing briefly. Davidson expressed that he was 'deeply mortified' by the incident. The controversy overshadowed the film's recognition, with Robert Aramayo winning the best actor award. BBC Chief Content Office

item.title: France Plans to Expand Nuclear Arsenal and Strengthen European Defense Cooperation
item.description: French President Emmanuel Macron announced that France will increase the number of its nuclear warheads and enable temporary deployment of nuclear-armed aircraft to allied countries for military exercises. The decision is part of a broader strategy to 'Europeanize' France's nuclear deterrence capabilities in response to evolving global security threats. Macron delivered this statement during a major speech at the Île Longue nuclear submarine base, emphasizing the involvement of several European countries, including Belgium, Denmark, Germany, Greece, Sweden, the Netherlands, Poland, and the United Kingdom. London and Berlin are expected to play especially important roles in coordinating the program. European Union leaders have already expressed interest in collaborating with France on the details of this initiative. Macron stressed that the move is necessary to strengthen Europe’s defense posture amid the disintegration of established rules governing the use of nuclear weapons and risin

item.title: Stephen Hibbert, Known for 'Pulp Fiction' Role, Passes Away at 68
item.description: Stephen Hibbert, the actor recognized for portraying 'The Gimp' in Quentin Tarantino's cult classic 'Pulp Fiction,' passed away at 68 in Denver, Colorado, on March 2, 2026, following a heart attack. His family is awaiting an autopsy to determine further details. Beyond his iconic role, Hibbert had a diverse career in entertainment, contributing as a writer for 'Late Night with David Letterman,' working on 259 episodes from 1984 to 1986. He also wrote for television series including 'Boy Meets World' and 'Mad TV.' In addition to 'Pulp Fiction,' Hibbert appeared in films such as 'Austin Powers: The Spy Who Shagged Me,' 'The Cat in the Hat,' and 'National Treasure: Book of Secrets.' In a 2024 interview, he reflected on the sudden fan recognition after 'Pulp Fiction's' release, describing the overwhelming attention he received. Hibbert was married to actress Julia Sweeney from 1989 to 1994 and remained connected with the film community. He is survived by his three children: Ronnie, Rosalin

item.title: Beyond Nuclear Condemns US and Israeli Military Strikes on Iran Amid Nuclear Negotiations
item.description:
Beyond Nuclear, a nuclear advocacy organization, issued a strong condemnation of the recent military attacks on Iran carried out by the United States and Israel, two nuclear-armed nations. The statement, released on February 28, 2026, argues that these strikes occurred at a critical moment when diplomatic negotiations were advancing toward a new nuclear verification agreement with Iran. International specialist Linda Pentz Gunter described the attacks as illegal, dangerous, and provocative, warning that they could escalate into a broader regional conflict, potentially involving nuclear weapons, with devastating global consequences.
The article notes that Iran, an NPT signatory, has consistently denied pursuing nuclear weapons and is permitted to develop civilian nuclear programs. Prior to earlier US-Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities in June (likely 2025), the IAEA had found no evidence of weapons development. Iran's current uranium enrichment is reported at 60% or possibly

item.title: Strait of Hormuz Closure Threatens Global Energy and Supply Chains
item.description: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical shipping route for the Persian Gulf, has emerged as a major threat to the global economy. Although stock markets like the S&P 500 and FTSE 100 have shown minimal movement, energy and supply chain experts warn that the economic impact will likely intensify over time. The shutdown is disrupting natural gas exports from Qatar, a key supplier, potentially triggering inflation in Europe and Asia as countries scramble to secure energy. The situation extends beyond gas; Qatar also produces a third of the world's helium, essential for MRI machines and certain quantum computers. The Gulf region is similarly affected, as limited pipeline capacity means that oil from Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq, and Abu Dhabi cannot be shipped efficiently, risking field shutdowns or spills. Additionally, the dependence of Gulf economies on foreign workers raises social concerns if migration patterns change due to safety threats. Overall, the longer the Strait of Horm

item.title: AI Liquid Biopsy Detects Early Liver Fibrosis and Cirrhosis Before Symptoms
item.description: Researchers at Johns Hopkins Kimmel Cancer Center have developed an AI-driven blood test capable of detecting early liver fibrosis and cirrhosis years before symptoms appear. The technology, called fragmentome analysis, examines genome-wide patterns of cell-free DNA (cfDNA) fragments circulating in the blood. Unlike traditional liquid biopsies that look for specific mutations, this method analyzes how DNA fragments are broken and distributed, capturing hidden signals of overall health. Using whole-genome sequencing of cfDNA from over 1,500 individuals with liver conditions and other medical issues, the AI system identifies fragmentation patterns linked to disease. This approach showed high sensitivity for early liver disease detection, offering the potential to intervene before fibrosis progresses to cirrhosis or increases the risk of liver cancer. The researchers also created a fragmentome-based comorbidity index, which predicted overall survival and could be applied to other chronic

item.title: Ultrafast Electron Transfer Driven by Molecular Vibrations in Solar Materials
item.description: Researchers at the University of Cambridge have observed electrons moving across solar materials in just 18 femtoseconds, revealing a process much faster than traditional theories predicted. The study shows that tiny vibrations in molecules act as a 'molecular catapult,' propelling electrons in a coherent, directional burst rather than allowing slow, random diffusion. The experiments involved a polymer donor placed next to a non-fullerene acceptor under conditions that were expected to impede charge transfer, yet electrons crossed the interface almost instantaneously. These vibrations mix electronic states, effectively pushing the electron across the molecular boundary and initiating a new vibration in the acceptor molecule. This ultrafast charge separation challenges long-standing assumptions in solar material design, which previously emphasized large energy differences and strong coupling for rapid electron transfer. The findings open up new design principles for more efficient light

item.title: Study Reveals a Massive Flat Structure of Matter Surrounding the Milky Way
item.description: Astronomers have long been puzzled by the fact that most galaxies near the Milky Way appear to be moving away rather than being pulled inward by its gravity. A new study from the University of Groningen, led by PhD graduate Ewoud Wempe, provides a compelling explanation. Using advanced computer simulations based on the early universe's conditions and measurements of the cosmic microwave background, researchers discovered that our galaxy sits within a gigantic, flat sheet of matter spanning tens of millions of light-years. This structure contains both ordinary matter and dark matter, while enormous cosmic voids lie above and below it. The simulations accurately reproduce the observed positions and velocities of galaxies near the Local Group, including the Milky Way and Andromeda. The flat distribution of mass within this sheet counterbalances the gravitational pull of the Local Group, allowing surrounding galaxies to drift outward. This virtual model, described as a 'twin' of our local

item.title: Four House Democrats Vote Against War Powers Resolution Amid Lobby Influence
item.description: On March 6, 2026, four Democratic members of the U.S. House of Representatives voted against a bipartisan War Powers Resolution proposed by Thomas Massie. The resolution sought to require congressional approval before any further military escalations, aiming to increase legislative oversight over decisions of war and conflict. These four Democrats, who have received substantial financial support from the pro-Israel lobby AIPAC, opposed the measure. According to reports, the lobbying group spent over $9.3 million supporting these representatives. The vote has sparked discussions about the influence of lobbying and campaign contributions on congressional decisions, highlighting concerns about the intersection of foreign policy, money in politics, and democratic accountability. Critics argue that the resolution’s failure reflects how lobbying can shape policy outcomes, while supporters of the legislators maintain that their positions reflect strategic or foreign policy considerations. Thi

item.title: Defense Contractors Commit to Boost Weapons Output After Iran Strikes
item.description: President Donald Trump announced that major U.S. weapons manufacturers have agreed to ramp up production to replenish munitions stockpiles depleted by recent military strikes on Iran. The White House hosted executives from leading defense firms, including Lockheed Martin and RTX (Raytheon’s parent company), to discuss accelerating output amid ongoing conflicts in the Middle East.
